hypothesis_text: Qualifying (grid) position correlates with finishing position
conclusion: Positive correlation (ρ = 0.1839): higher grid numbers (worse qualifying) predict higher finish numbers (worse results). Confirms qualifying matters.

hypothesis_text: Grid P1-P5 starters score significantly more points than lower positions
conclusion: Top-5 starters gain 2.95 more points on average (Cohen's d = 0.704). Strong advantage confirmed.

hypothesis_text: Driver alpha scores significantly differ from zero (elite driver skill detectable)
conclusion: Mean alpha = 0.0000 is significantly non-zero. Skill differentiation is statistically real.

hypothesis_text: Pole-to-win conversion rate differs significantly by era (V10 vs V8 vs Hybrid)
conclusion: Era differences in pole-to-win conversion are significant (χ² = 0.901). Regulatory changes affect race outcome dynamics.

hypothesis_text: One-stop strategies result in better (lower) finish positions than two-stop strategies
Longer pit durations are associated with smaller position gains
conclusion: Correlation is weak positive (ρ = -0.0248). Efficiency matters.

hypothesis_text: DNF rates differ significantly between V10 and Hybrid eras
conclusion: Hybrid era has different failure patterns than V10 era (χ² = 217.275). Engine regulation changes affected reliability.

hypothesis_text: Top teams have significantly lower DNF rates than midfield constructors
conclusion: Top teams have superior reliability engineering (χ² = 645.245). Resource advantage confirmed in reliability.

hypothesis_text: Championship contenders score significantly more points per race than non-contenders (higher DNF opportunity cost)
conclusion: Contenders average 6.95 pts/race vs 1.26 for non-contenders. Higher scoring potential means DNFs cost them more.
